content.text: What Regulatory Compliance Initiatives Are

Azure ships built-in policy initiatives that map Azure controls to industry compliance frameworks such as CIS Microsoft Azure Foundations, NIST SP 800-53, PCI DSS, ISO 27001, FedRAMP, and HIPAA. Each initiative is a policySetDefinition that groups hundreds of policy definitions under named control domains using the policyDefinitionGroups feature. When assigned to a subscription or management group, the initiative drives the Regulatory Compliance dashboard in Microsoft Defender for Cloud, showing per-control and per-domain compliance percentages.

attrs.alt: Regulatory compliance initiative architecture: frameworks → initiative → compliance dashboard

content.text: These built-in initiatives are read-only — Microsoft manages them and releases new versions periodically. They cannot be modified in place. When a specific control or policy within a built-in initiative does not apply to your environment, you disable it at assignment time via an effect parameter override without touching the definition itself. For frameworks that require evidence packages for auditors, the compliance dashboard exports control-by-control compliance reports.
Assigning a Built-in Regulatory Compliance Initiative

# List all built-in regulatory compliance initiatives
az policy set-definition list \
  --query "[?policyType=='BuiltIn' && metadata.category=='Regulatory Compliance']
         .{Name:name,DisplayName:displayName}" \
  -o table

# Common initiative IDs (stable — these are Microsoft's published IDs):
# CIS 2.0:          06f19060-9e68-4070-92ca-f15cc126059e
# NIST 800-53 Rev5: 179d1daa-458f-4e47-8086-2a68d0d6c38f
# PCI DSS 4.0:      c676748e-3af9-4e22-bc28-50fed30136f7
# ISO 27001:2013:   89c6cddc-1c73-4ac1-b19c-54d1a15a42f9
# FedRAMP Moderate: e95f5a9f-57ad-4d03-bb0b-b1d16db93693

# Assign CIS 2.0 at management group scope
az policy assignment create \
  --name cis-benchmark \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --policy-set-definition 06f19060-9e68-4070-92ca-f15cc126059e \
  --params '{
    "logAnalyticsWorkspaceId": { "value": "/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-monitoring/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/law-corp" }
  }'

# The initiative has many parameters — use the portal to discover them all
az policy set-definition show \
  --name 06f19060-9e68-4070-92ca-f15cc126059e \
  --query 'parameters' -o json | head -100

# Trigger a compliance scan to populate results
az policy state trigger-scan --management-group mg-corp

# View compliance summary for the CIS assignment
az policy state summarize \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --filter "policySetDefinitionId eq '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policySetDefinitions/06f19060-9e68-4070-92ca-f15cc126059e'" \
  --query 'results'
Disabling Non-Applicable Controls

Built-in regulatory compliance initiatives contain hundreds of policies, and not all are relevant to every organization. For example, a company using Entra ID P2 with PIM already satisfies certain access control requirements through compensating controls outside Azure Policy's visibility. In these cases, suppress the noisy policy using an effect parameter override at assignment time or an exemption for specific resources.

# Find parameter names for effect overrides in a built-in initiative
# Parameter names follow: effect-<policyDefinitionReferenceId>
az policy set-definition show \
  --name 06f19060-9e68-4070-92ca-f15cc126059e \
  --query 'parameters | keys(@)' -o json | grep effect

# Disable a specific policy (e.g., CIS 3.1 - Storage account public access check)
# by overriding its effect parameter at assignment update
az policy assignment update \
  --name cis-benchmark \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --set 'parameters.effect-a60aMStoRageaCcountsMustRestrictNetworkAccess.value=Disabled'

# Alternative: create an exemption for a specific subscription that has compensating controls
az policy exemption create \
  --name exempt-sub-storage-access \
  --scope /subscriptions/$LEGACY_SUB_ID \
  --policy-assignment /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/cis-benchmark \
  --exemption-category Waiver \
  --description 'Legacy subscription uses separate firewall appliance for storage traffic filtering' \
  --expires-on 2027-01-01T00:00:00Z

# View exemption impact on compliance score
az policy state summarize \
  --subscription $LEGACY_SUB_ID \
  --filter "policySetDefinitionId contains '06f19060'" \
  --query 'results.resourceDetails'
policyDefinitionGroups: Mapping Policies to Control IDs

The policyDefinitionGroups array in a regulatory compliance initiative maps control framework identifiers (such as CIS 3.1, NIST AC-2, PCI DSS Requirement 7) to sets of Azure policies. This mapping powers the compliance dashboard — each policy carries a groupNames list that references one or more policyDefinitionGroups entries, and the dashboard aggregates compliance by group (control domain). Custom initiatives can adopt the same structure to create audit-ready compliance reports for internal frameworks.
Regulatory Compliance Scoring

The compliance percentage shown in the Defender for Cloud Regulatory Compliance dashboard is calculated per control and per overall initiative. The formula is straightforward: (compliant resource count) / (compliant + non-compliant resource count) for resources in scope. Exempt resources are excluded from the denominator, which means exemptions improve the compliance percentage. Not Started (Disabled) resources are also excluded, since they are not evaluated.
Compliance State
Counts in Denominator?
Counts as Compliant?
Effect on Score
Compliant
Yes
Yes
Increases score
NonCompliant
Yes
No
Decreases score
Exempt (Waiver/Mitigated)
No
No
Excluded from calculation
Not Started (Disabled)
No
No
Excluded from calculation

# Compute compliance percentage manually for a subscription
RESULTS=$(az policy state summarize \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --filter "policySetDefinitionId eq '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policySetDefinitions/06f19060-9e68-4070-92ca-f15cc126059e'" \
  --query 'results.resourceDetails')

COMPLIANT=$(echo $RESULTS | jq '[.[] | select(.complianceState=="Compliant") | .count] | add')
NONCOMPLIANT=$(echo $RESULTS | jq '[.[] | select(.complianceState=="NonCompliant") | .count] | add')
TOTAL=$((COMPLIANT + NONCOMPLIANT))
PCT=$(echo "scale=1; $COMPLIANT * 100 / $TOTAL" | bc)
echo "CIS Compliance: ${COMPLIANT}/${TOTAL} (${PCT}%)"

# Generate a per-control compliance breakdown (mimics the Defender dashboard)
az graph query -q "
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.policySetDefinitionId == '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policySetDefinitions/06f19060-9e68-4070-92ca-f15cc126059e'
| mv-expand group = properties.policyDefinitionGroupNames
| summarize
    Compliant    = countif(properties.complianceState == 'Compliant'),
    NonCompliant = countif(properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant')
    by controlId = tostring(group)
| extend CompliancePct = round(100.0 * Compliant / (Compliant + NonCompliant), 1)
| order by CompliancePct asc
" --management-groups mg-corp
Exporting Compliance Evidence for Auditors

# Export a full compliance report for a regulatory initiative
# Method 1: Azure Policy compliance export (CSV) via Portal
# Navigate to: Policy > Compliance > select initiative > Download Report

# Method 2: Export via REST API (for automation)
az rest \
  --method post \
  --uri "https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.PolicyInsights/policyStates/latest/summarize?api-version=2019-10-01" \
  --body '{
    "filter": "policySetDefinitionId eq \'/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policySetDefinitions/06f19060-9e68-4070-92ca-f15cc126059e\'"
  }' > compliance-summary.json

# Method 3: Resource Graph export to CSV
az graph query -q "
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.policySetDefinitionId contains '06f19060'
| project
    resourceId     = tostring(properties.resourceId),
    subscriptionId = subscriptionId,
    resourceGroup  = tostring(properties.resourceGroup),
    controlId      = tostring(properties.policyDefinitionGroupNames[0]),
    state          = tostring(properties.complianceState),
    policyRef      = tostring(properties.policyDefinitionReferenceId)
| order by controlId asc, state asc
" --management-groups mg-corp --output table > compliance-evidence.csv

# Method 4: Defender for Cloud regulatory compliance workbook
# In Azure Monitor Workbooks, import the Regulatory Compliance workbook template
# (available in the Azure Monitor Workbook gallery) for a structured report
# with charts by control domain, subscription, and time period.
Keeping Regulatory Initiatives Up To Date

Microsoft releases new versions of built-in regulatory compliance initiatives when the underlying frameworks are updated or when new Azure services introduce relevant controls. The policySetDefinition ID stays the same between versions, but the policy definitions included change. There is no automatic assignment migration — you must update the assignment to adopt the new initiative version. In practice, this means re-assigning or using the Azure Policy initiative version feature if the portal exposes an upgrade path.
Action
Frequency
Method
Check for new initiative versions
Quarterly or when framework publishes new version
az policy set-definition show and compare metadata.version
Update assignment to new version
When Microsoft releases a new built-in version
Re-create assignment with --policy-set-definition pointing to latest
Add new controls that appear in updated initiative
When initiative version adds new policy definitions
Re-assign initiative — new policies in the definition are automatically included
Export and archive compliance evidence
Before any audit or reporting period
REST API export or Resource Graph query to CSV
Review exemptions
Quarterly
az policy exemption list --scope; purge expired or unnecessary exemptions
Terraform: Assigning a Built-in Regulatory Compliance Initiative
Resource Graph: Comprehensive Regulatory Compliance Queries

// Per-control-domain compliance breakdown for CIS initiative
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.policySetDefinitionId == '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policySetDefinitions/06f19060-9e68-4070-92ca-f15cc126059e'
| mv-expand group = properties.policyDefinitionGroupNames
| where isnotempty(group)
| summarize
    Compliant    = countif(properties.complianceState == 'Compliant'),
    NonCompliant = countif(properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'),
    Exempt       = countif(properties.complianceState == 'Exempt')
    by controlId = tostring(group), subscriptionId
| extend Total = Compliant + NonCompliant
| where Total > 0
| extend CompliancePct = round(100.0 * Compliant / Total, 1)
| order by CompliancePct asc

// Which subscriptions have the lowest compliance score?
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.policySetDefinitionId == '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policySetDefinitions/06f19060-9e68-4070-92ca-f15cc126059e'
| summarize
    Compliant    = countif(properties.complianceState == 'Compliant'),
    NonCompliant = countif(properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant')
    by subscriptionId
| extend Total = Compliant + NonCompliant
| where Total > 0
| extend CompliancePct = round(100.0 * Compliant / Total, 1)
| order by CompliancePct asc
| take 10

// Find exemptions that are about to expire (within 30 days)
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.Authorization/policyExemptions'
| where properties.policyAssignmentId contains 'cis-v2'
| where isnotempty(properties.expiresOn)
| where todatetime(properties.expiresOn) < now() + 30d
| project
    exemptionName = name,
    scope         = id,
    expiresOn     = todatetime(properties.expiresOn),
    category      = tostring(properties.exemptionCategory),
    description   = tostring(properties.description)
| order by expiresOn asc

// Resources that became non-compliant in the last 7 days (new violations)
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.policySetDefinitionId contains '06f19060'
| where properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'
| where todatetime(properties.timestamp) > ago(7d)
| summarize count() by
    policyRef = tostring(properties.policyDefinitionReferenceId),
    subscriptionId
| order by count_ desc
Multi-Framework Assignment Strategy

Organizations typically need to satisfy multiple compliance frameworks simultaneously. Assigning five different initiatives to the same scope causes the same resources to be evaluated multiple times — once per initiative. Each evaluation is independent, and compliance states are tracked per initiative per resource. The platform's cost scales with the total number of policy evaluations (resources × policies per initiative), so avoid assigning frameworks that have significant policy overlap unless audit requirements genuinely require separate reporting.

# Assign multiple regulatory initiatives at MG scope
# CIS v2.0
az policy assignment create \
  --name cis-v2 \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --policy-set-definition 06f19060-9e68-4070-92ca-f15cc126059e \
  --params @cis-params.json

# NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5
az policy assignment create \
  --name nist-800-53-r5 \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --policy-set-definition 179d1daa-458f-4e47-8086-2a68d0d6c38f \
  --params @nist-params.json

# PCI DSS 4.0 (assign at subscription scope for payment card environments only)
az policy assignment create \
  --name pci-dss-4 \
  --subscription $PCI_SUB_ID \
  --policy-set-definition c676748e-3af9-4e22-bc28-50fed30136f7 \
  --params @pci-params.json

# List all regulatory compliance assignments across MG hierarchy
az policy assignment list \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --disable-scope-walk false \
  --query "[?contains(policyDefinitionId, 'policySetDefinitions')]
         .{Name:name,Scope:scope,Initiative:policyDefinitionId}" \
  -o table

# Check combined policy evaluation count for a subscription
az policy state list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --query 'length(@)' \
  -o tsv
# Large count (50k+) can increase scan latency in very large environments
Using Defender for Cloud Recommendations to Fix Compliance Gaps

When the regulatory compliance dashboard shows non-compliant controls, Microsoft Defender for Cloud links each control to specific recommendations that describe what is wrong and how to fix it. Each recommendation corresponds to one or more policy definitions in the initiative. Remediation steps from Defender for Cloud recommendations are the operational bridge between the compliance report and the actual fix — either a manual change or a policy remediation task.

# List all unhealthy (non-compliant) recommendations in a subscription
az security assessment list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --query "[?properties.status.code=='Unhealthy']
         .{Name:displayName,Severity:properties.metadata.severity,Resource:id}" \
  -o table

# Find which recommendations map to a specific CIS control
# (CIS 3.1 maps to storage account public access policy)
az security assessment-metadata list \
  --query "[?contains(userImpact, 'CIS')]
         .{Name:displayName,Policy:policyDefinitionId}" \
  -o table

# Bulk remediate a specific type of violation via policy remediation task
# First: find the policyAssignmentId and policyDefinitionReferenceId
az policy state list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --filter "policySetDefinitionId contains '06f19060' and complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --query '[0].{Assignment:policyAssignmentId,RefId:policyDefinitionReferenceId}' \
  -o json

# Create remediation task for DINE/Modify policies within the initiative
az policy remediation create \
  --name remediate-cis-vm-agent \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --policy-assignment /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/cis-v2 \
  --definition-reference-id auditVmLawAgent \
  --resource-discovery-mode ReEvaluateCompliance
Summary

Regulatory compliance initiatives map built-in Azure Policy definitions to control framework identifiers using policyDefinitionGroups. Assigning one of these initiatives at management group scope drives the Defender for Cloud compliance dashboard, providing per-control and per-domain compliance scores. Suppress non-applicable controls using effect parameter overrides at assignment time, and use exemptions for resources with compensating controls. Export compliance evidence via the REST API or Resource Graph for auditor review. Custom initiatives can adopt the same policyDefinitionGroups structure to build compliance reporting for internal frameworks.
